findings.recommendation: In org-badges/route.ts POST, check the actor is assigned to (ownerType, ownerId) before createOrgBadge (super-admin bypass). In DELETE, load the badge and verify `canApplyOrgBadge(id)` (or an equivalent assignment check) before deleteOrgBadge. Mirror the exact authorization used by the bulk-apply path.

findings.title: Changelog notify marks notifiedAt only after the full per-recipient fan-out — a mid-entry timeout re-emails everyone
findings.evidence: notifyNewChangelogEntries loops entries, and for each entry sends to ALL recipients sequentially (`for (const to of recipients) await sendChangelogEntryEmail(...)`) and only AFTER that inner loop does `db.update(changelogEntries).set({notifiedAt}).where(id=r.id)`. The route caps at maxDuration=120s. There is no per-recipient send-ledger.
findings.impact: If the function is killed (timeout / cold-start eviction) partway through an entry's recipient list, that entry's notifiedAt is never set, so the next cron run re-sends the entry to the WHOLE list — duplicate changelog emails to every subscriber. Severity scales with subscriber count.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) mark notifiedAt before the recipient loop and accept rare under-delivery, or (b) record per-(entry,recipient) sends in a small ledger and skip already-sent pairs on retry. Option (b) is the idempotent fix consistent with the rest of the codebase's ledger pattern.

findings.title: Inbound claim-reply webhook does not bind the reply to the original recipient (content trust)
findings.evidence: recordInboundReply matches a thread purely by `requestNumber` parsed from the subject (claim-thread.ts:96-98) and stores fromEmail/body without verifying the sender equals the thread's original toEmail. request_number is a `serial` (schema.ts:1404), i.e. sequential/guessable. The only thing preventing forged inbound mail is the Svix signature (resend/route.ts:42-57, fail-closed, timing-safe in svix-verify.ts:42).
findings.impact: Low in practice: forging an inbound POST requires the RESEND_INBOUND_SIGNING_SECRET, so an external attacker cannot inject. But anyone who legitimately receives ANY claim email could reply with a different request number in the subject and have their message appended to another claim's admin-visible thread (cross-thread content spoofing in the Claim Review Console). The admin sees the real fromEmail, so it is detectable, not silent.
findings.recommendation: In recordInboundReply, also compare normalized fromEmail against the thread's most-recent outbound toEmail and flag/drop mismatches (or store a `senderVerified` boolean the console surfaces). Keep returning 200 to avoid Resend retry storms.

summary: Build hygiene is largely strong: single pnpm lockfile is enforced everywhere (CI, changelog action) with --frozen-lockfile, tsconfig is strict with no ts-ignore escape hatches, next.config does not suppress build/TS errors, all 6 cron routes have matching vercel.json schedules with sane maxDuration values, and the every-minute crons use FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED so overlap is safe. The real weaknesses are governance/gating: the "BLOCKING" test gate and the changelog sync are both conditional on repo vars (silently skip if unset), a 71-error lint backlog keeps lint non-blocking, scripts/ is excluded from typecheck so the changelog-sync Action ships untypechecked code, a debug DB-seed file was committed to the repo root, and ~10 real secret-bearing env vars are missing from .env.example.

findings.title: Debug DB-seed script committed to repo root
findings.evidence: _tmpseed.cjs is git-tracked (git ls-files --error-unmatch _tmpseed.cjs succeeds; committed in 005ee8d "Event Followups — all 6 phases (#195)"). It loads .env.local and runs raw INSERTs against whatever DATABASE_URL is present: `const sql = neon(process.env.DATABASE_URL_UNPOOLED || process.env.DATABASE_URL); ... await sql\`insert into evaluations (...) values ('https://linkedin.com/in/_tmpf', 'Tmp Founder', 90, ...)\``. It seeds fake 'Tmp Founder'/'Tmp Investor' evals and attaches them to real approved attendees of event slug 'tfo1tkl3'.
findings.impact: A throwaway seed script that mutates evaluations/event_attendees shipped to main. If anyone runs `node _tmpseed.cjs` with prod creds in .env.local it injects bogus 90/70-scored profiles into a live event. It also signals .gitignore (`.env*` / `*.local` is covered, but ad-hoc .cjs at root is not) doesn't catch loose debug artifacts.
findings.recommendation: Delete _tmpseed.cjs from the repo. If a seed fixture is genuinely needed, move it under scripts/ (already gitignored for data, and excluded from the typecheck), guard it with a non-prod DB-host assertion like tests/global-setup.ts does, and never reference prod-capable DATABASE_URL by default.

findings.title: scripts/ excluded from typecheck → changelog-sync Action ships untypechecked code
findings.evidence: tsconfig.json `"exclude": ["node_modules", "scripts/**/*"]` — so `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit` (the only BLOCKING gate in ci.yml) never type-checks anything under scripts/. Yet changelog-sync.yml runs `pnpm exec tsx scripts/build-changelog.ts 40` against the PROD changelog DB (`CHANGELOG_PROD_DB_URL`) and `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` on every push to main. build-changelog.ts also imports from src/db/schema.ts, so a schema rename can break the script with zero CI signal.
findings.impact: A type error or a schema-drift mismatch in build-changelog.ts (or any scripts/* used by the Action) is invisible to CI and only surfaces as a failed prod changelog-sync run after merge — exactly the drift the comments warn about (dev.to/Neo/v0.0.8 rubric lag).
findings.recommendation: Add a lightweight typecheck of scripts to CI, e.g. a second `tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.scripts.json` that includes scripts/ (the main app tsconfig can keep excluding them for build speed). At minimum typecheck build-changelog.ts since it's invoked in an automated prod-touching Action.

findings.title: .env.example drift: ~10 real secret-bearing vars undocumented
findings.evidence: The example header tells you to regenerate the used-var set, but the file has drifted. Vars referenced in code but absent from .env.example include secrets: RESEND_INBOUND_SIGNING_SECRET (gates the inbound webhook — src/app/api/inbound/resend/route.ts:42), BLOB_READ_WRITE_TOKEN (Vercel Blob uploads — sponsors/hosts/event-photo routes), CHIEF_API_TOKEN/CHIEF_PROJECT_ID (src/lib/chief.ts), GOOGLE_API_KEY (google-kg/youtube enrichers), ENRICHLAYER_API_KEY (src/lib/enrichlayer.ts), LIBRARIESIO_API_KEY, CLAIM_REPLY_TO, plus rate-limit knobs ADMIN_API_PER_DAY_LIMIT, API_EVENTS/INDUSTRIES/SEARCH_PER_DAY_LIMIT, CLAIM_VERIFY_PER_DAY_LIMIT, ENRICHER_TIMEOUT_MS, CONCURRENCY.
findings.impact: A fresh deploy or contributor following .env.example silently ships without inbound-webhook verification configured (the route then rejects all claim replies — see its own comment) or without Blob uploads/event enrichers working. Most fail closed (good), but the gap defeats the file's stated purpose and hides a security-relevant secret (RESEND_INBOUND_SIGNING_SECRET).
findings.recommendation: Re-run the documented `grep -rhoE "process\.env\.[A-Z_0-9]+" src/ scripts/ | sort -u` and reconcile. At minimum add RESEND_INBOUND_SIGNING_SECRET and BLOB_READ_WRITE_TOKEN under REQUIRED/feature sections; group the new rate-limit and enricher-credential vars with the existing optional blocks. Consider a CI step that fails on drift to keep it honest.

findings.title: "BLOCKING" test gate is conditional on a repo var — silently skips if unset
findings.evidence: ci.yml job `test` is labeled "BLOCKING test gate" but carries `if: ${{ vars.ENABLE_CI_TESTS == 'true' }}`. A skipped job reports success to required-status-checks, so if ENABLE_CI_TESTS is ever unset (or removed) the gate evaporates with no red X. The same pattern gates changelog-sync (`vars.ENABLE_CHANGELOG_SYNC == 'true'`). Additionally vitest.ci.config.ts permanently EXCLUDES 5 suites (rescore-all, eval-pipeline, select-top-profiles, profiles-scored, hn-tokenmaxxing) — including the 1328-LOC eval-pipeline orchestrator — so even when enabled, the pipeline's core test never runs in the gate.
findings.impact: The protection most relied on for the scoring/credits surface can be turned off by a single repo-variable change with no visible failure, and the highest-risk pipeline suite is excluded by design. Combined this means the 'blocking' gate gives weaker coverage than its label implies.
findings.recommendation: Make the test job unconditional and instead fail fast with a clear message if TEST_DATABASE_URL is missing, OR add a required "gate-summary" job that asserts the test job actually ran (not skipped). Prioritize per-test isolation for tests/lib/eval-pipeline.test.ts so it can rejoin the gate (it's the orchestrator that touches credits + spend).

findings.title: 71 lint errors block lint from ever becoming a real gate
findings.evidence: ci.yml lint job is `continue-on-error: true` ("informational"). Full `pnpm run lint` reports 110 problems (71 errors, 39 warnings). Error classes: 22 @next/next/no-html-link-for-pages (using <a> instead of next/link for internal nav — e.g. src/components/admin/NotAuthorized.tsx:28, EventHostPicker.tsx:38), 21 @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any, 19 @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars, 19 @next/next/no-img-element, 13 react-hooks/static-components, 10 @typescript-eslint/no-require-imports, 4 react-hooks/set-state-in-effect, 1 prefer-const. Note 32 eslint-disable comments already exist in src/ but zero @ts-ignore/@ts-expect-error.
findings.impact: Lint can never be promoted to a required gate while 71 errors stand, so genuinely meaningful rules (react-hooks/set-state-in-effect can cause render loops; no-html-link-for-pages breaks client-side nav/prefetch) keep slipping in. The any/require-imports/unused-vars classes are noise that masks the real ones.
findings.recommendation: Bucket the fix: (1) auto-fixable (prefer-const, some unused-vars) via --fix; (2) mechanical (no-html-link-for-pages → next/link, no-img-element → next/image) in one sweep; (3) the 4 react-hooks/set-state-in-effect are real bug-risk — review individually. Then drop continue-on-error so lint becomes blocking (it's already quick-win #5 in the audit doc).

findings.title: Cron secret compared with non-constant-time ===
findings.evidence: isAuthorizedCron compares the bearer token with a plain string equality: `return auth === \`Bearer ${secret}\`;`. JS `===` on strings short-circuits on the first differing byte, leaking a (small) timing signal. The Stripe and Resend webhook paths correctly use library signature verification (constructEventAsync, verifySvix), so this is the one hand-rolled secret comparison.
findings.impact: Low: CRON_SECRET is high-entropy and the per-route global rate-limit/circuit-breakers blunt brute force, but a constant-time compare is the standard and cheap. The localhost bypass is already correctly restricted to non-production.
findings.recommendation: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length buffers (guard length first) for the secret compare, mirroring the pattern already trusted in svix-verify.
